figures.caption: Figure 2. Round time breakdown across model scales. Blue bars: client training (Ttrain); orange bars: server aggregation (Tagg). The PS is idle during the entire training phase.
Figure 3. Lambda aggregation time breakdown. S3 reads (blue) dominate across all models, accounting for 91–99% of total time. FedAvg compute (orange) is 1.3–2.9 s regardless of model size.
Table I COMPARISON OF SERVERLESS FL AGGREGATION APPROACHES. N : CLIENTS PER ROUND, |θ|: MODEL PARAMETERS, M : NUMBER OF GRADIENT SHARDS, k: CLIENTS PER LEAF AGGREGATOR, L1 : LEVEL-1 AGGREGATOR COUNT.
Figure 4. Cost breakdown per round: Lambda compute (memory × time) vs. S3 I/O (3NM +M operations), M ∈ {1, 2, 4, 8, 16}.
Table V SHARD ABLATION FOR GRADSSHARDING, N = 20, 10 ROUNDS.

Figure 1. The three serverless FL aggregation architectures (schematic; actual aggregator counts depend on N and branching factor). Orange boxes (Λ) are Lambda functions; green boxes are S3 buckets. In all three architectures, the final aggregated gradient is written back to S3. (a) λ-FL: two-level tree, each Lambda holds the full gradient. (b) LIFL: three-level hierarchy, all inter-level communication via S3, each Lambda holds the full gradient. (c) GRADSSHARDING: M parallel Lambdas each average one shard across all N clients; each holds only |θ|/M in memory.
